Atlanta, Georgia, the headquarters of...
We are in downtown Atlanta.

A little bit.

I know that Chelsea from Webflow introduced you.
I didn't actually get to make the connection.
Like, how do you know Chelsea over there?
So, yes, we do.
We are, I suppose, an enterprise customer of Webflow.
We're about to enter the second year of a third of a three-year agreement.
I have been incredibly transparent with them that I'm not a huge fan at the moment, but we are working on some things.
And I think in kind of spirit of partnership, when I was chatting with them, they offered to kind of hook me up with, I think you.

And another agency that they've worked with as we're rebuilding our site in Q2, or we're starting now to have it rolled out by Q2, that we're sort of positioned in a unique way to really kind of really look at how we approach, I won't say AEO, I'll say all optimizations of any search type engine, because we all know there's TEO and AEO and SEO and LLMs and AIO and all the things.
So anyway, they offered to hook me up with a couple other agencies.
So that led me to you and I think one other that I'm going to meet with to see kind of what your areas of expertise are.
I think they've seen some success in working with a couple of the agencies.
And so just sort of exploratory.
Okay, awesome.
Yeah, we work closely with Webflow as a client at GrowthX.
And we do create like a lot of SEO and GEO content on their behalf, but it's not...
Just like editorial, right, content that would live on the blog, we create like a lot of web closed docs and integrations pages in that particular use case.
In terms of like growthx high level, think of us differently from a typical agency because we're end-to-end, right?

We can do SEO and GEO strategy, come up with a roadmap of assignments, and then we try to publish or refresh content every single business day so that you can really create quality at scale and, you know, quantity is so important with AI search and the ways that people can come in with all this nuance to the way that they're searching.

And so we'll even do like the LLM visibility and monitoring so that you see where you're showing up, how that's progressing, and what you might want to think about most in terms of topic coverage that your competitors might be doing a better job with.
When you think about like content creation today for SEO and GEO, what does that team look like?
How many folks are involved?
Is it all in-house?
Tell me just like a little bit more about the research.
Very limited.
In the past, it's been a mixture of in-house and external.
We split our company two years ago and, in essence, became a smaller company out of that split.
So now NCR doesn't exist.
You have NCR Atleos and NCR Voyix.
So NCR Voyix is a significantly smaller company than NCR.
Obviously, it was split.
And so in doing that, I think we've looked and realigned a lot of our resources and said, okay, how do we be more efficient?
So today, our content team has gotten smaller and smaller.
It's really like two, I think, in-house.
And we've used various agencies over the years to help supplement content.
I think there's also exploration around how do we create more strategic roles in-house and leverage tools like AI, energetic AI and the AI capabilities that we have within.
Um, mostly Microsoft is what we're using today.
So, um, to help augment that.
It's a very small team, very lean.
I mean, how many folks are we talking that create content on the blog today?
Just two?
Yep.
But that's all content.
So they're not even focused specifically on blogs.
So oftentimes, like today, I can tell you, we don't have any current blogs because they're focused on other things at the moment.
And so what are the agency partners, um, executing on?
There's none right now.
So right now, not creating content.
Right now, the vast majority of our agencies are focused.
Well, let me, let me pause on that.
Um, we, I have an agency that focuses on website build.

It's page.

All those things.

But the agency that's working on the website is going to be creating content there.
We have another agency that worked on the relaunch and the repositioning, but they're not writing content, evergreen content like blogs or docs or anything that is ongoing in that way.
have a PR team that does press releases.

So we have different agencies that are doing different things.
None, no single agency is focused on content creation.
They're creating content within the silos of what their expertise is.
So, for instance, another one, we have ABM, where we'll have a campaign agency that's helping with content to support a specific campaign.
Makes sense.

That's a good question.

So the answer for my purview is yes.
I need to create content that lives in digital spaces that help improve our performance in LLMs, in AIO, in SEO, in GEO, like all of those things.
Her strategy, right, might be slightly different because she's looking at it from a brand and content perspective and I'm looking at it from a yes.
How do I write the content?
How much content do we need?
Blogs specifically because that lives within the digital space.
I would say that's probably my primary content pillar.
But then her purview might be something different, right?
They're working on customer stories.
They're working on press releases.
They're working on brand positioning pieces, obviously, in conjunction with blogs as well.
I also have social media, so we're always looking for thought leadership.
Again, things that repurpose on digital spaces in order to drive that top of funnel, mid-funnel, bottom funnel, all of those things.
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense because typically we work as like an extension of your team.
And when you don't have a lot of resources to create content on the website, you can't get close to publishing every business day or refreshing every business day.
And I think looking through the blog, I saw maybe one article every week or one article every two weeks.

So we could definitely help the team scale with quality and then free up some time so that they could focus on maybe more social media or maybe more pillar pieces of content that comes really from your in-house expertise.
Right.
As opposed to a partner.
But our approach is really interesting because we have.
We phases in our engagements.

The first phase is this eight-week strategy sprint, and the whole focus is first generating these artifacts around your company, your audience, your brands, and then creating calibration articles and a roadmap of assignments so that you can see the quality we can hit and also the topics that we would cover in terms of content assignments in that growth execution where we look to publish every business day.
And so, you know, with our approach, we can ingest resources that you have around your brand, around your messaging, around your products, right?
And then we can speed up the process at which we develop the context that makes the AI workflows that we use super effective.
Because I think you mentioned, like, today, the team isn't really using any AI tools to create content, right?
I would say at the elementary level, right?
But yes, they are, but more at
At a very, very entry level, we have not gotten into from the marketing perspective, leveraging, working, creating AI agent flows.
That is kind of next phase.
And a lot of it has to do with our internal, let's see, we'll just call it minutiae between legal and everybody else.
It is very difficult for us to get any AI tools opened up from a marketing perspective.
We're starting to leverage it in different areas.
So I'm hopeful that in 2026, we can.
But today, it's very elementary.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And like what we've seen is that using those tools, it can be pretty steep in terms of like a learning curve.
So think about like a tool like Aerox, right?
Like you have to go in and you have to figure out how to stitch together these workflows.
And then update if the models ever change.
And then like, what would the costs look like if you're using all these different.
API calls, right, and different platforms to do your research.
So a lot of times, like, our benefit can be the time to balance, you know, instead of having, yeah, go ahead.
Let me ask you a quick question.
Back to your phase one, the AEO strategy.
You said inputs from, you said brand.
What were the three again?
Yeah, it's really brand, audience, and then company.
Okay.
So these can be product marketing one-pagers.
These can be any sort of work you've done around your ICP.
But it's just essentially, like, who's writing the content?
Who are we writing it for?
And how do we want it to sound, right?
Resources that help our team understand that quickly.

And as part of your business model around building those sort of AI workflows or those agentic AI workflows that go in and tap into resources that would provide you with that information around the brand, the audience, and the company.
I'll give you an example, right?
I'm kind of a geek around anything.

Anything AI podcast, so I listen to them all the time, right, is creating something that goes out and looks at our docs site, which is an index site, but it's mostly around documentation of the product.
ICP is, like you said, would be something that would be internal, right?
And ICP is an internal document that lives within our system.
It's not public facing.
And then you have brand, which brand positioning also would be an internal document, not public facing.
And then I say company because there could be things around product or whatnot that would be public facing that could be pulled from like the website or other indexed places.

So is sort of the model that you guys use, because I heard you say AI workflows, so if they go in and you say you have agents that are built that say, go grab all this information and create content using it, that is then edited or vetted by the humans, right, before it is published?
Yeah, absolutely.
Yeah, So, you know, what we have with our solution here.
I'm going to share.
Let at my screen and show you a couple of slides.
We've got an internal platform that powers all the work that we do with experts in the loop, right?
So in this internal platform, we've built these AI workflows, and then we feed the context into them and can customize them from client to client to make them more relevant.
I'll show you an example of like what it looks like as we create a piece of content.
But then with all of our clients, we've got a big delivery team of like over 60 resources that all come from SEO, content marketing backgrounds.
Our chief content officer was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for like over a decade.
And so the idea is like combining the best of both worlds.
And then as we work with our clients, and most of our clients, you know, they're B2B software companies.
Some are very technical and appreciate how this engine that we've built can create content that's more technical in nature.
But what we're doing, you know, is we're in the first phase going...
And then looking at, you know, what is the opportunities that we can go out and execute on?
So keyword analysis, search volume, difficulty of ranking against specific competitors.
For a client like yourself, we would look at all the different content that you've created on your site today.
And we'd start to navigate, you know, what are the different refresh opportunities or what are the different types of content that we can go in and create?
And then all of the content that we create will be focused on these different topic clusters that are relevant to the topics where you want authority, right?
So topics where you want to show up in the answer.
And so, you know, from there, we're actually generating these assignments and then creating drafts that all your team has to do is just review, right?
Give us feedback.
And then we handle the staging and publishing.
We handle all the analytics and then help you kind of orient to what's performing well, where we should focus our strategy.
Um.

But all the while, you know, we'll have like a massive backlog of assignments so that you can sort of point us in the right direction or we can lead you, right, in terms of like where should we create content?
So, you know, for Lulvable right now, there's 136 ideas that are ready to go, and we just want the client to give us a thumbs up to start executing on, you know, these assignments.
And then, but as far as building the content, right, I get that.
And I'm assuming not only is it keywords, it's prompts and things like that, right?
Absolutely.
Yeah.
And then, you know, as far as the actual content building goes, how is it, how are you generating that content?
So I get, I get what you've done here, which is I've identified areas where I can build content.
So I'll give you an example, right?
So we've been dabbling in the SEMrush AI, you know, LLM sort of tracking tool just to play around with it, right?
And so it'll give me what my prompts are.
I could put my prompts down, link at my competitors, see prompts that are actually generating brand results and links versus just, you know, some sort of result, right?
That there might be actionable brand results, and then it'll give me content that I can create.
How are you guys differentiating for something like that?
Like, are you using, again, going back to my question, getting access, because this is a, from my procurement standpoint, I have to understand this, right?
Are you getting access to certain assets within our library that then is creating this content using an AI agent or somebody physically writing it?
a human being writing it, or are you using AI to write it?
Yeah, absolutely.
We're using AI to create an initial draft, and then using a human to revise that draft into something that is as tailored to your brand as possible, right?
And your audience as possible.
So...
As part of our process, in phase one, we're generating these artifacts.
So if you look at Engine, they're in a business travel space.
We've created all these business travel guides.
We've got artifacts around their company context.
And so we're using resources that they send us to create these artifacts.
Not like feeding the resources into AI, but for our own internal understanding.
The workflows that we would use to create artifacts like these, they're not ingesting any resources that you send, right?
They're doing research online about your company.
And then we're using this in addition to like our kickoff call to inform what these artifacts said.
And so we'll create this around the company.
We'll create custom artifacts, sometimes around like personas and different industry verticals.
But the idea is that every piece of content that we create, it's feeding these artifacts in as context.
Who's writing the article?
Who is it written for?
And then what is the voice?
Right.
What the article needs?
to have when it's written, but we've also got like artifacts that are more rule-based.
So there's this proofreader checklist where we're giving specifics about, you know, what not to mention or how to, you know, which terms to use, which resources to use as we go out and do research.
And then if you look at like one of these artifacts here, or sorry, one of these workflows to create this brief for this assignment, we're feeding in the artifact around the persona.
And then we're also feeding in the artifact around the company with some context from our managing editor around the article that's being written, right?
And then there's also a keyword here.
So, or a topic right there, right?
So the keyword or a topic is we're writing a comparison article about three different tools.
And so to generate this output, you know, we are going in and having this.
Workflow execute on several steps in parallel.
What's the keyword traffic for the relevant keywords on this assignment?
What are the organic results?
What are the surf ranking factors, the competitor content, the search intent?
And then all of those steps that it goes through lead to the output that you see here.
But then what we do to create a piece of content is we feed this brief into a workflow where we then go in and do all this deep research, right?
When we generate a draft, we generate a final article, our team could basically come in and make changes to the content.
They could select like a portion and then maybe say like, you know, insert three bullet points after that are written for a backend developer.
And so this is how the humans and like the AI are like working together, right?
As they're making the content.
Thank you.
Okay, then it's just my question.
And so then, you know, if you take any of these individual steps here, because we built all of our workflows in code, you know, there's several things happening in the background for the execution.
So for the research, you know, we went and looked at that.
What we're doing is we're taking all these inputs and then we're generating a research platform.
So, you know, is it strict?
Is it exploratory?
Is there a specific audience that we're writing for?
Is there an objective or questions that we need to answer?
And so then it's going to go out and execute this research in a manner where, you know, if we don't meet like the criteria, we'll rerun the research.
So it's agentic in that sense.
This is just an example where, you know, on the first run through, we had a quality score that met the criteria.
And so we didn't run this research again.
just stored the research and the knowledge base.
And then.

And so, you know, we're doing everything from crosslinking to taking what we've written and validating it, validating it, so the writing guidelines, we're taking our entire article and fact-checking it, so we'll split up the article into different passages and then go and do deep research to validate that the content's accurate.

If it's not accurate or if there's, like, not a certain confidence interval, we'll suggest a rewrite, and so what our editors do is, you know, they make these decisions about how to accept this rewrite, right?

They think about, like, what is the topic that we're writing for and how can I refine this article to be just, you know, a better answer to that ultimate question, you know, that it's being written to answer.
And then we're also creating, like, cover images, infographics, and then doing all this.
Staging and publishing on our client's behalf.
So, you know, everything that you see here, like, we'll actually handle going in and staging and publishing it in Webflow for you.
Got it.
And then you mentioned LL on visibility or, I guess, KPIs, metrics.
How is, and by the way, this all, I'm assuming there's different types of content.
There's short form, long form.
You'd mentioned that you, that not pillar pieces.
It's, I'm assuming, predominantly just short and long form kind of articles, blogs.
Correct.
Yeah.
Primarily editorial, but we've also done programmatic.
So, you know, for Ramp, we've created this entire vendor directory and populated each vendor page with their own internal spend data.
And so we could execute on a programmatic use case.
We've done, like, the integrations pages for Webflow.
So, you know, that's a bit different than editorial, long form, short form.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
But we always start there as the foundation, because that's what's going to get cited in AI answers, right?
It's going to be your blogs.
Okay.
Okay.
All right, let's talk through, which I think I certainly understand.
I want to make sure that I'm going summarize here, is that, I know we've got like five minutes.
So, I'm just starting to have conversations with agencies that are doing work around leveraging AI workflows and agentic AI to produce output.
And so, I'm starting to start to ask the questions and dig in a little bit differently than I have with agencies in the past, right?
Which is, how many people are writing it?
How are you approving it for SEO?
And, you know, all those things.
It's definitely a learning curve, but it's interesting.
So, it sounds like the value of an agency like yours.
And.

The next ones that I'm going to talk to are going to be around the fact that you have shortened that learning curve because, and I mean this in the kindest way, if we were smart enough and had the resources, this is nothing that we couldn't do ourselves, meaning I could go out and, you know, get my agentic, you know, AI app and build a workflow and go tell it to go to Google Docs or wherever and pull this and do all the things that you just did.
But it's going to take me a long time because I'm not in the same, I'm not, don't have the same experience and learnings that you guys do.
So the value you're bringing is that you've already done that work, you've vetted it, you have all these clients that it's proven for.
Is that kind of an accurate way to sum it up?
Yeah, I think you're definitely not wrong.
I'm not going to like push back and say that's not true.
I think this is, this is basically the workflows that we built are coming from like a marketing leader who has always leveraged content and organic.
That's the way of driving growth.
And so it's not to say your team couldn't do it, but it's coming from basically someone who's had success building this at every step in his career.
And I can tell you more about like our CEO, Marcel, but the idea is that we're leveraging multiple models and we're breaking it into distinct steps.
So the first step is like generating an assignment.
And the next step is generating a brief.
And then the next step is doing deep research on that brief and thinking about the outline and thinking about the first graph.
And then, you know, can you take that and then also reference it with custom writing guidelines or custom instructions?
Can you take that and then fact check every sentence in the article?
And so, you know, I think the team could build something that is comparable, but maybe not as comprehensive in terms of like every step that we're using in part of the content creation process.
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah, it makes perfect sense.
Yeah.
It's amazing to me that we're in this space now.
It's amazing
Um, it's crazy.
Yeah, it definitely is.
That I'm literally having conversations with agencies that have been doing this for some time.
I'm like, where was I?
We're clearly behind.
We're behind in general.

We're not staffing to build AI agents.
That's just not what we're doing, right?

We're trying to figure out how do we have the right partners to put in place, like GrowthX, to help us do that so that we can continue to scale and do what we do best, which is go to market with our products.
So I get it.
I'm just, I'm literally, you're one of the first that I've talked to.
I obviously have a couple of others that, so I'm learning as well.
So I appreciate that transparency.
And the truth is, like, yes, if we wanted to go and hire a bunch of people and try to get the experts and create all the stuff, it would take a very long time.
I get it.
For sure.
And it's basically just being able to go from maybe one blog every week or every other week to...
Five articles every single week at the quality of the one article that's being written right every one to two weeks so that you can give yourself more shots on both.
Yeah.

Does it also, does the, I'm assuming that the workflow or the, you know, agent also is capable of, like you just said, reading the KPIs and determining what it is that's performing well and optimizing to create in that step that you were just talking about content that fits more in that.
It's like optimizing it, I guess, to Google AdWords, right?
Hey, this is working really well.
Let's do more of that.
Yeah, definitely.
For a client like yourself, we would refresh a lot of the content that you've written as part of, like, the quick wins that we would find.
You know, we recommend, like, refreshing every three months.
And it's not just, like, adding an FAQ section, right?
It's thinking about, like, is this content up to date and still relevant?
You know, is there an FAQ section?
Is there good cross-linking?
If there's not, you know, can we scan your entire site to figure out where it makes sense to cross?
So it's things like this that we do with existing content in addition to the net new content we create.
Got it.
Okay.
So I guess my question, well, everybody's question, my question is going to be, you know, what does a partnership look like?
Are you guys on retainer?
Is it based on deliverables?
What does it look like?
Yeah, for sure.
And then I got to run to another call, but we can definitely set up more time to chat.
So our partnership, it's two phases.
The first phase is a 30K strategy sprint.
It's eight weeks.
We typically publish like 10 to 20 articles by the end of the strategy sprint.
So it's not just strategy, right?
We're also going to publish.
And then it moves into a 12-month term where, again, we're really just trying to scale quality.

And so we focus all of our energy in phase one on developing the artifacts so that we have the context engineering for the workflows, developing those calibration articles, and then publishing, right?
20 pieces.

And then in phase two, you know, you're able to opt out before phase two, but you can see the quality we can hit and all the content that we focus on creating in the first three to six months, and then make like an educated decision on whether you want to take that, you know, 12 month commitment with growthx from there.
Got it.
Okay.
And then I know we got to go, but I'd love to, and maybe I'll, let me, let me get through a couple more meetings and I'll probably have different questions.

Like if I'm paying, you know, $15,000 to $18,000 a month, we have to align on what success looks like.
Totally.
Makes, makes complete sense.
Yeah.

Okay.
We can definitely dig deeper into like the analytics because I know we didn't talk too much about that.
Okay.
Perfect.

Awesome.

Good to meet you.
You too.
